narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated an 89% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile shows strong points like an LSAT score of 37, which is a bit above the typical student's 36.7, and coming from an upper-middle-income family, along with being a white male planning full-time studies—traits common among applicants. However, your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 sits slightly below the average of 3.21, and more concerningly, you ranked in the bottom decile of your school for both Year 1 and Year 3 grades, far below the middle-of-the-pack averages around 5.7 and 5.5. When the model weighed all this, your Year 1 class ranking had the strongest pull toward predicting failure, nudging the odds higher for not passing the bar. Right behind it was your LSAT score, which surprisingly also tipped the scales slightly toward failure in the model's eyes for someone with your overall profile. Your Year 3 ranking came next in influence, followed by family income, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status, with each playing a smaller but still failure-leaning role. None of your features strongly argued for success here, creating a cumulative picture of risk. Compared to typical students who pass, you're in the lowest tier for those early and later school rankings, which often signal challenges in handling rigorous legal coursework and bar prep. To boost your future chances, focus on rebuilding your academic foundation—perhaps through targeted GPA improvement via post-bac courses or retaking undergrad classes if possible, since low deciles like yours are red flags for bar struggles. Strengthening study habits early, like those reflected in your Year 1 performance, could make a big difference for the bar. Consider LSAT retakes or prep programs tailored to your full-time path, as even your solid score didn't offset the rest. With effort on these realistic steps, you could shift your profile toward the successful students who ranked higher in school and maintained stronger GPAs. Hang in there—this is a data-driven call, but your determination can change the story.
